Name: dynamic_gpu_kernel_optimization
Title: Dynamic GPU Kernel Optimization Based on Real-Time Resource Profiling
Short Hypothesis: By implementing a dynamic profiling system that adjusts GPU kernel configurations in real-time based on occupancy and memory bandwidth metrics, we can achieve significant improvements in inference throughput without requiring architectural changes.
Related Work: Current kernel optimization efforts like Astra and KRISP focus on static or pre-defined configurations. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a dynamic approach that adapts to real-time conditions, leveraging hardware profiling to inform kernel configurations on-the-fly, thus maximizing resource utilization and minimizing latency.
Abstract: Recent advancements in GPU kernel optimization have primarily focused on static configurations that fail to adapt to varying workload conditions during inference. In this proposal, we introduce a novel dynamic GPU kernel optimization framework that utilizes real-time resource profiling to modify kernel parameters on-the-fly. By monitoring key performance metrics such as occupancy, memory bandwidth utilization, and warp stalls, our framework will dynamically adjust tile sizes, kernel launch parameters, and memory access patterns to optimize throughput under fixed accuracy budgets. We will leverage existing CUDA/Triton kernel infrastructure to implement this approach, ensuring that all modifications are hardware-faithful and reproducible. Our experiments will demonstrate that this dynamic adjustment leads to measurable improvements in latency and throughput compared to static configurations, thus providing a more efficient and flexible solution for deploying large language models on commodity GPUs.
Experiments: Conduct baseline measurements using static kernel configurations to establish a performance benchmark.
Implement the dynamic profiling system and conduct A/B testing against the baseline, measuring wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second under varying workloads.
Evaluate the impact of real-time adjustments on occupancy rates, memory bandwidth utilization, and warp stall reasons, collecting detailed profiling data using nvprof/Nsight.
Test the effectiveness of different profiling intervals and adjustment thresholds to identify optimal dynamic tuning parameters.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The dynamic approach may introduce complexity that could lead to overhead in profiling and adjustment, possibly negating some gains.
Real-time profiling might not be feasible under all workload conditions, particularly for very low-latency scenarios.
The proposal may require extensive experimentation to identify optimal tuning parameters for various model architectures.
